title: Tungsten
content:
Tungsten, or wolfram, is a chemical element with the symbol W and atomic number 74. Tungsten is a rare metal found naturally on Earth almost exclusively as compounds with other elements. It was identified as a new element in 1781 and first isolated as a metal in 1783. Its important ore s include tungsten, scheelite, and wolframite, the last lending the element its alternate name.

The free element is remarkable for its robustness, especially the fact that it has the highest melting point of all the elements discovered, melting at (carbon sublimes rather than melts at atmospheric pressure). It also has the highest <a href="boiling%20point" boiling point</a, at.

Its density is 19.25 grams per cubic centimetre, comparable with that of <a href="uranium" uranium</a and <a href="gold" gold</a, and much higher (about 1.7 times) than that of <a href="lead" lead</a.

Polycrystalline tungsten is an intrinsically <a href="brittle" brittle</a and <a href="hardness" hard</a material (under standard conditions, when uncombined), making it difficult to <a href="metalworking" work</a. However, pure single-crystalline tungsten is more <a href="ductility" ductile</a and can be cut with a hard-steel <a href="hacksaw" hacksaw</a.
